title: Active Multi-Front War: SAF-RSF Conflict Escalates in Kordofan and Darfur
summary: The war between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) entered its fourth year with no sign of resolution, with fighting escalating across multiple fronts. As of early March 2026, an alarming escalation of violence was reported in the Kordofan region, including a drone strike on Dilling General Hospital killing at least four patients, and continued drone attacks on El Obeid over five consecutive days (UN News, 5 March 2026). The Security Council Report (February 2026) noted that Kordofan has emerged as a key epicentre of hostilities, while fighting persists in North Darfur and Sennar states. ACLED (9 March 2026) confirmed the RSF continues to move into North Darfur, and a 25 January clash in Blue Nile state opened a new front, placing Blue Nile at the centre of the conflict.

title: De Facto State Fragmentation and RSF Parallel Government
summary: The RSF's proclamation of a parallel 'Government of Peace and Unity' (Tasis State) in August 2025, based in Darfur and allied with the SPLM-N, has deepened Sudan's political fragmentation. The ICG warned that without a diplomatic breakthrough, Sudan risks shifting from all-out war to a protracted stalemate and de facto partition (ICG, September 2025). The SAF-aligned government returned national institutions to Khartoum in January 2026 (Security Council Report, February 2026), while the RSF consolidates control over most of Darfur and parts of Kordofan. The UN and Arab League recognise SAF chief al-Burhan as head of state, but the African Union does not, deepening the legitimacy vacuum and complicating future governance frameworks.

title: Mass Displacement Crisis: Largest in the World
summary: Sudan hosts the world's largest displacement crisis, with approximately 14 million people forcibly displaced since April 2023. Per UNHCR (October 2025), this includes 7.3 million internally displaced persons and 4.2 million refugees and asylum seekers in neighbouring countries including Egypt, Chad, South Sudan, Ethiopia, Uganda, Libya, and the Central African Republic. UNHCR's 2026 Sudan Regional Refugee Response Plan, launched in February 2026, sought $1.6 billion to support 5.9 million people across seven neighbouring countries, and projected an additional 470,000 new refugees expected in 2026 (UNHCR, February 2026). Around 2.6 million displaced Sudanese have returned to areas such as Khartoum, Sennar, and Al Jazirah, often driven by hardship rather than safety, finding destroyed infrastructure and absent services (UNHCR, 2025).

title: Famine Confirmed and Spreading in Darfur and Kordofan
summary: Famine (IPC Phase 5) has been confirmed in El Fasher (North Darfur) and Kadugli (South Kordofan), with IPC projecting 20 additional areas at risk of famine across Greater Darfur and Greater Kordofan through 2026 (FAO/WFP/UNICEF, November 2025). An IPC alert issued 5 February 2026 found that famine thresholds for acute malnutrition had been surpassed in the additional North Darfur localities of Um Baru (53% of children under five acutely malnourished) and Kernoi (approximately one-third) (IPC/Al Jazeera, February 2026). WFP estimates 24.6 million people suffer from acute hunger and 2 million face famine or risk of famine, and urgently requires $700 million to continue Sudan operations from January to June 2026 (WFP, 2026). The humanitarian response plan for Sudan in 2026, requiring $2.9 billion, had received only 5.5% of necessary funds as of early February 2026 (Action Against Hunger, February 2026).

title: Systematic Attacks on Civilians, Sexual Violence, and Impunity
summary: Both the SAF and RSF continue to commit war crimes, including mass killings, sexual violence, and unlawful detention. Following the RSF seizure of El Fasher in October 2025, there were immediate reports of extrajudicial killings and serious violations against fleeing civilians, with the UN Fact-Finding Mission (FFM) identifying hallmarks of genocide (HRW World Report 2026, February 2026). The UN has documented at least 330 cases of conflict-related sexual violence, with the actual number believed to be far higher due to access restrictions and stigma (HRW, 2026). The UN Security Council in late February 2026 condemned all forms of violations against civilians and expressed alarm over repeated drone attacks on civilians and humanitarian workers, including strikes on WFP operations since early February 2026 (Al Jazeera, February 2026). Impunity remains a key driver of violence, with both parties refusing to cooperate with the UN FFM and AU Joint Fact-Finding Mission.

title: Deliberate Obstruction of Humanitarian Access
summary: Both warring parties continue to wilfully obstruct aid movement, attack humanitarian workers and local volunteers, and restrict access to conflict-affected populations, in violation of international humanitarian law (HRW, February 2026). The UN Security Council in February 2026 condemned repeated drone attacks targeting WFP operations and stressed that starvation must not be used as a weapon of war (Al Jazeera, 25 February 2026). The UN Security Council's 1591 Sudan Sanctions Panel of Experts, whose mandate expired in March 2026, had not yet been constituted due to disagreements among Council members, limiting accountability mechanisms (Security Council Report, September 2025). As of February 2026, UN OCHA confirmed that humanitarian access is becoming increasingly restrained, particularly in Kordofan and Darfur (UN News, 5 March 2026).
keywords: ['aid obstruction', 'humanitarian access', 'WFP attacks', 'RSF', 'SAF', 'OCHA', 'sanctions panel', 'starvation weaponized', 'IHL violations']

title: Collapsed Health System and Renewed Disease Outbreak Risk
summary: Sudan's health system has been driven to the brink of collapse, with 37% of health facilities remaining non-functional as of January 2026, over 20 million people requiring health assistance, and WHO verifying 201 attacks on healthcare facilities since April 2023, resulting in 1,858 deaths (WHO, 9 January 2026). Sudan declared the end of its major cholera outbreak on 3 March 2026, with 124,418 infected and 3,573 deaths since July 2024 (WHO EMRO, 8 March 2026), though the underlying drivers of disease transmission remain fully intact. Ongoing outbreaks of dengue, malaria, and measles continue to spread, fuelled by overcrowded displacement sites, disrupted WASH services, and collapsed immunization (WHO, January 2026). The risk of renewed cholera and other waterborne disease outbreaks is high given persistent displacement, damaged water infrastructure, and upcoming rainy season.

title: Regional Proxy Dynamics and External Military Involvement
summary: The Sudan conflict has become increasingly shaped by external actors, with the UAE widely reported as extensively supplying weapons and logistics to the RSF, and Egypt providing logistical and technical support to the SAF, including reportedly conducting targeted airstrikes against the RSF (Amani Africa, February 2026). The AU Peace and Security Council has strongly condemned external actor involvement but made no progress in over two years in identifying and sanctioning those responsible (Amani Africa, February 2026). Saudi Arabia and the UAE are reportedly locked in an intensifying proxy competition, with Riyadh backing the SAF and Abu Dhabi the RSF, making the Quad framework effectively non-functional as a diplomatic convenor (Washington Institute, 2026). Turkish firms have also reportedly sold weapons to both sides in violation of US and EU sanctions (FDD, February 2026).

title: Stalled Peace Diplomacy and Fragmented Mediation Architecture
summary: Multiple parallel peace initiatives — the US-led Quad (Egypt, Saudi Arabia, UAE, US), the AU Quintet (AU, UN, EU, Arab League), and bilateral Egyptian efforts — have failed to produce a ceasefire as of March 2026, with their overlapping mandates generating coordination challenges rather than unified pressure. The AU Quintet was welcomed at the last AUPSC meeting as a coordination mechanism, but Saudi-UAE rivalry has rendered the Quad largely ineffective (Washington Institute, 2026). A Quad-proposed peace plan in early 2026 — including humanitarian truce, ceasefire, and a political process toward civilian-led government — was rejected by the SAF citing UAE involvement (Amani Africa, February 2026). The UN Panel of Experts supporting the 1591 Sudan Sanctions Committee had not been constituted as of early March 2026 due to Council member disagreements, undermining accountability and early-warning functions (Security Council Report, September 2025).

title: Severe Humanitarian Funding Gap Threatening Aid Operations
summary: Sudan's 2026 humanitarian response plan requires $2.9 billion but had received only 5.5% of required funds as of early February 2026, and barely 40% of required funding was secured in 2025 (Action Against Hunger, February 2026). WFP has been forced to reduce food rations to survival minimums and urgently requires $700 million for January to June 2026 operations (WFP, 2026). UNHCR's 2026 Sudan Regional Refugee Response Plan launched a $1.6 billion appeal for 5.9 million people across seven neighbouring countries, warning that funding per refugee per month had dropped from $11 in 2022 to $4 in 2025, forcing closure of two of three registration centres in Egypt (UNHCR, February 2026). The UNFPA was forced to withdraw from more than half of the 93 health facilities it supported due to funding cuts, and three-quarters of all safe spaces for women and girls in South Sudan hosting Sudanese refugees have closed (UNHCR, February 2026).

title: Cross-Border Conflict Spillover to Neighbouring States
summary: Reports of South Sudanese nationals captured fighting in RSF ranks in North Kordofan were confirmed by SAF sources in January 2026, highlighting active cross-border recruitment and the internationalisation of the conflict (Al Jazeera, 3 January 2026). The Security Council Report (February 2026) flagged concerns about cross-border alliances and support networks, including the use of neighbouring countries as transit routes for weapons, raising risks of wider regional destabilisation. IOM estimated that over 8,000 people were displaced from North Darfur's Kernoi locality in early January 2026, with many crossing into Chad (UN News, January 2026). Sudan's conflict continues to drive secondary crises in host countries, with Uganda, Chad, Egypt, and South Sudan under severe strain from refugee influxes amid their own pre-existing vulnerabilities.

title: Arbitrary Detention, Political Repression, and Civil Society Suppression
summary: Both warring parties continue to unlawfully detain, torture, and execute civilians. A local rights group reported in September 2025 that the SAF and its allies were detaining over 3,000 people in Gezira state, most of them political activists, while in April 2025 over 25 women faced possible death sentences for alleged RSF collaboration without due process (HRW World Report 2026, February 2026). The SAF committed retaliatory attacks against local emergency response volunteers accused of collaborating with the RSF following the recapture of Khartoum in March 2025 (HRW, 2026). Sudan's civilian political space has further contracted as both the SAF-aligned Port Sudan government and the RSF-backed Tasis administration prioritise military consolidation over inclusive political transition, marginalising civilian democratic forces and deepening the governance vacuum.
